matches: experience logged only after measured improvement
gaps: not yet mutating train.py with an LLM
not yet an overnight autonomous loop
not yet training a real model architecture

formulas.J: J = 1.20 * correctness + 0.95 * speed_score + 0.20 * memory_bonus + 0.15 * stability - 0.18 * complexity - 0.05 * (line_count / 10)

task_catalog.title: Optimize contains_duplicates
task_catalog.description: Improve a naive quadratic duplicate detector with deterministic correctness checks and a real benchmark.

task_catalog.title: Optimize first_repeated_value
task_catalog.description: Preserve correctness for the first repeated item while replacing the quadratic scan with a streaming strategy.

task_catalog.title: Optimize has_overlap
task_catalog.description: Turn a nested-loop overlap check into a real set-based benchmarked optimization.

task_catalog.title: Optimize most_frequent_item
task_catalog.description: Replace a quadratic mode finder with counted architectures and compare one-pass versus two-pass strategies.

task_catalog.title: Optimize deduplicate_preserve_order
task_catalog.description: Keep original order while removing duplicates, and compare hash-based versus order-breaking architectures.

task_catalog.title: Optimize missing_number
task_catalog.description: Find the missing integer with architecture families that mimic search, sorting, and arithmetic reasoning.

task.description: Improve a naive quadratic duplicate detector with deterministic correctness checks and a real benchmark.

plan.objective: Improve a naive quadratic duplicate detector with deterministic correctness checks and a real benchmark.

baseline.label: Initial program
baseline.strategy: Use the checked-in baseline implementation without any optimization.
notes: This is the starting point that every candidate must beat.
The baseline is executed exactly like the mutated candidates.

candidates.label: Streaming seen-set
candidates.strategy: Use a seen set so later tasks can reuse the same pattern for first-repeat and overlap problems.
notes: Slightly more code than cardinality check but more reusable across task families.
This is the memory-friendly pattern for later tasks.

candidates.label: Set cardinality check
candidates.strategy: Materialize a set and compare its size with the original list length.
notes: Uses the Python runtime's optimized hash table.
Usually the fastest option when the whole container must be scanned anyway.

candidates.label: Sort and scan neighbors
candidates.strategy: Replace the nested loop with a sorted pass and an adjacent comparison.
notes: Correct and deterministic.
Cuts the search from O(n^2) to O(n log n).

winner.label: Streaming seen-set
winner.strategy: Use a seen set so later tasks can reuse the same pattern for first-repeat and overlap problems.
notes: Slightly more code than cardinality check but more reusable across task families.
This is the memory-friendly pattern for later tasks.

baseline.label: Initial program
baseline.strategy: Use the checked-in baseline implementation without any optimization.

The baseline is executed exactly like the mutated candidates.

candidates.label: Replay-guided streaming set
candidates.strategy: Reuse the duplicate-detection memory and return on the first repeated value seen in order.
notes: Carries a concrete memory forward from the first task.
Fast and preserves the original traversal order.

candidates.label: Count then rescan
candidates.strategy: Build counts once, then rescan to find the first value whose count exceeds one.
notes: Correct and clearly faster than the nested baseline.
Needs two passes through the list.

candidates.label: Sorted adjacency guess
candidates.strategy: Sort the values and take the first adjacent duplicate, which is fast but wrong for the original order semantics.
notes: This candidate is intentionally incorrect for first-occurrence semantics.
The evaluator should reject it even if it is fast.

winner.label: Replay-guided streaming set
winner.strategy: Reuse the duplicate-detection memory and return on the first repeated value seen in order.
notes: Carries a concrete memory forward from the first task.

baseline.label: Initial program
baseline.strategy: Use the checked-in baseline implementation without any optimization.
notes: This is the starting point that every candidate must beat.
The baseline is executed exactly like the mutated candidates.

candidates.label: Replay-guided smaller-side set
candidates.strategy: Use replay memory to materialize only the smaller side as a set and stream the other collection through it.
notes: Generalizes the hash-membership lesson from earlier tasks.
Avoids unnecessary work when one side is much smaller.

candidates.label: Two-set intersection
candidates.strategy: Materialize both sides as sets and test whether the intersection is non-empty.
candidates.code:
def has_overlap(left, right):
    return bool(set(left) & set(right))
notes: Concise and usually fast for medium-sized collections.
Builds two sets even when one side is much smaller.

candidates.label: Sort and merge
candidates.strategy: Sort both collections and walk them with two indices.
notes: A solid O(n log n) fallback when hashability is uncertain.
More moving parts than a hash-based check.

winner.label: Replay-guided smaller-side set
winner.strategy: Use replay memory to materialize only the smaller side as a set and stream the other collection through it.
notes: Generalizes the hash-membership lesson from earlier tasks.
Avoids unnecessary work when one side is much smaller.

baseline.label: Initial program
baseline.strategy: Use the checked-in baseline implementation without any optimization.
notes: This is the starting point that every candidate must beat.
The baseline is executed exactly like the mutated candidates.

candidates.label: Counter most_common
candidates.strategy: Use Counter and delegate the selection logic to the standard library.
notes: Short and expressive.
Usually wins when library overhead is dominated by the quadratic baseline.

candidates.label: One-pass running best
candidates.strategy: Reuse counted-structure intuition and maintain the current best answer during the counting pass.
notes: Moves selection into the same pass as counting.
Acts like a lightweight architecture search over counting styles.

candidates.label: Two-pass counting table
candidates.strategy: Build a frequency table and then select the maximum count.
notes: A direct architectural upgrade over the quadratic baseline.
Separates counting from selection.

winner.label: Counter most_common
winner.strategy: Use Counter and delegate the selection logic to the standard library.
notes: Short and expressive.
Usually wins when library overhead is dominated by the quadratic baseline.

baseline.label: Initial program
baseline.strategy: Use the checked-in baseline implementation without any optimization.
notes: This is the starting point that every candidate must beat.
The baseline is executed exactly like the mutated candidates.

candidates.label: Streaming seen-set dedup
candidates.strategy: Reuse hash-membership memory and explicitly preserve order with a seen set and output buffer.
notes: More explicit than dict.fromkeys and easier to generalize.
This is the replay version of stable deduplication.

candidates.label: Sorted set
candidates.strategy: Sort a set of values, which is fast but breaks the original order guarantee.
notes: This candidate intentionally violates task semantics.
The evaluator should reject it like Karpathy would discard a faster but invalid run.

candidates.label: dict.fromkeys
candidates.strategy: Exploit insertion ordering in dict to preserve order and remove duplicates.
notes: Compact and usually very fast.
Uses language-level insertion ordering as the architecture trick.

winner.label: Streaming seen-set dedup
winner.strategy: Reuse hash-membership memory and explicitly preserve order with a seen set and output buffer.
notes: More explicit than dict.fromkeys and easier to generalize.
This is the replay version of stable deduplication.

candidates.label: Replay-guided set lookup
candidates.strategy: Reuse hash-membership memory and check membership with a set instead of scanning the list repeatedly.
notes: Bridges the set-logic family into a numeric task.
Not the most elegant architecture, but replay makes it available.

candidates.label: Arithmetic checksum
candidates.strategy: Subtract the observed sum from the expected arithmetic progression.
notes: An architecture-level change from search to algebra.
Most faithful to Karpathy's idea of genuinely trying a different architecture.

candidates.label: Sort and detect gap
candidates.strategy: Sort the values once and scan for the missing integer gap.
notes: A classical sorting architecture.
Correct but heavier than arithmetic reasoning.

winner.label: Replay-guided set lookup
winner.strategy: Reuse hash-membership memory and check membership with a set instead of scanning the list repeatedly.
notes: Bridges the set-logic family into a numeric task.
Not the most elegant architecture, but replay makes it available.
